The goal is always to be involved and consistent, whether that means finishing tasks, learning something new, or helping with a project.The quote also shows how actions can change outcomes. People do better work when they are interested in it, and they keep their jobs when they do better work.Putting these ideas together makes it clear that actions lead to results. It shows how the things you do every day can have a long-term effect on your career.
Article Summary: Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia, has been involved in the growth of graphics technology, advanced computing, and artificial intelligence. "Don't get bored and don't get fired" can be seen as a simple way to work. It says that people should stay involved in their jobs and do what their company expects of them. Being engaged at work is very important for growth and productivity.

Article Title: In 2006, Jack Dorsey saw one SMS inform an entire group and reimagined the way the world shares news
Article Text:
The man whose quiet observation rewired the way the world communicates. Image Credits: Wikimedia Commons

It began with the way we already texted

How 140 characters were, in fact, a feature

Twitter as a Tool for Health Research: A Systematic Review

Twitter sketch by Jack Dorsey. From a rough drawing to hundreds of millions of users. This is where it started. Image Credits: Wikimedia Commons

One update, a whole network

From one SMS to a cultural habit





Before the retweet, before the trending tab, before Elon Musk and the rebrand to X, there was a pretty unremarkable idea, one that Jack Dorsey had sometime around 2006. He saw something small but powerful. One text message could tell a bunch of people what's going on, right away. No e-mail threads. No phone tag. A quick burst of text and all were up to speed.That simple observation would become the backbone of one of the most influential platforms in the history of the internet.The genius of early Twitter was not about inventing something new. It took something millions of Americans already do, like sending a quick text message, and made it a social, public experience. Instead of updating one person, you could update all your followers at once, in real time.This is what product thinkers sometimes call making status a product. A status update is not a long blog post or a well-written email. They're short, fast, light, and weirdly satisfying to send and receive.In a study in the Journal of Medical Internet Research , Twitter is described as a status-update microblogging service from its early days, and that framing was not accidental.It was never meant to compete with blogging or long-form journalism. The idea was to catch the quick, immediate pulse of everyday life.One of those design decisions that sounds like a constraint but was actually a gift is the character limit. When you can only say so much, you have to pick your words. No long-winded setup time. You either get to the point, or you don't post.The study,, confirmed that Twitter, launched in 2006, had a compact 140-character format and that this structure was not incidental. It was the product itself. The limitation made Twitter feel native on mobile phones when touchscreens were not yet a thing and typing on a numeric keypad was still the norm. That short text seemed natural, and Twitter went all out on that.There was also a psychological benefit. Posting seemed low-stakes because the messages were short. You were not writing an essay. You were just speaking. This lowered the cost of participation, and people posted more, making the whole network livelier and more useful.What really makes Twitter different from a group text is scale. One text to ten people, twenty maybe. A tweet could reach hundreds, thousands, or millions if it went viral. That difference in reach changed how people thought about what was worth sharing.Suddenly, ordinary moments were being watched. A funny thing on your way to work this morning, a live TV event hot take, breaking news from someone on the ground; Twitter allowed any user to be, in some small way, a broadcaster. The platform didn’t invent that impulse; the desire to share something is profoundly human, but it provided that impulse with a delivery system it never had before.Later, the concept was extended to characterise tweets as status updates, suggesting that the original framing remained relevant even as the platform grew. The format grew, but the basic logic remained.The nice thing about the Twitter origin story is its lack of pretension. There was no bold vision to reinvent global communications. It was just a man who saw that a short text could get the word out to a group really fast, and he thought, what if that was a product? That single observation led to something that helped shape political movements, sped up the delivery of breaking news, gave celebrities a direct line to fans and changed the way Americans follow live events, from Super Bowls to election nights to anything in between.The platform on which millennials and Gen Z grew up tweeting, live-posting concerts, games and finales wasn’t built on some complex technological breakthrough. It was built on an existing habit, a little simpler, a lot more social.
Article Summary: The genius of early Twitter was not about inventing something new. It took something millions of Americans already do, like sending a quick text message, and made it a social, public experience. A status update is not a long blog post or a well-written email. They're short, fast, light, and weirdly satisfying to send and receive.
